author: @WhatsThePointOfIt
text:
The linear motion is going to be L*sin(t) where t is the angle.t = 45 gives 1/2, t = 0 gives 0, t = 90 gives L.

So if one wants to calculate some specific length then it's that simple.

What one would likely want to do is rather than having the length change by the crank, automate the crank using a motor or stepper.  One could also automate the direction of the linear motion giving essentially complete control through automation.

author: @jamieoxenreider
text: I just made the same thing,-the first bit with the idea of a crank for an engine-I'm like you have been tinkering all my life and at 47 actually I realized 20yrs ago-it's waste of time. So many times I spent massive amounts of time inventing stuff only to see it was made and even more often I invent stuff and 10 or 20 yrs later I see it on tv but its a flop even though it was a good idea. I can honestly say to young science lovers and inventers-Just don't, it's all been invented or will be and they will ripe you off out of it. You can invent if your part of a billion dollar company with a team working on something so brilliant the average person can't so it isn't patented yet. I have invent probably 3 dozen engines and probably 2 dozen already have either patent or I beat others out by a decade but not to the patent or process. I still have 2 that are awesome because here I see someone beat me to this one. Young people, forget about it, you will waste 1/2 your life thinking and scrounging for bits an pieces, trying to build a machine to do what you can't afford or dreaming of buying something only to find out its not going to help. I gave up but every few years the bug hits. Please walk away.

author: @1992jamo
text: I was thinking about this all morning, and did some calculations but unfortunately it can't be used as a CVT. However, it then hit me. You've actually invented something even cooler! The continuously variable clutch! A clutch system that is infinitely smooth, and doesn't even use friction! The X and Y rails could even be replaced with a scotch yoke for simplicity.

replies.author: @1992jamo
replies.text:
​@areadenial2343 100% sure. So lets focus on the X axis movement only. As the assembly is rotated clockwise, more and more of the vertical stroke length is reduced and the horizontal stroke length is increased.
It turns out that mathematically, there isn't any mechanical advantage when the stroke length is short, the power output is just lower.
The axis that travels less distance reaches it's maximum position at the same time that the other axis also reaches it's maximum range. They are both phase synced, but there is no mechanical advantage for the axis which is moving a smaller distance. 
So, imagine only the horizontal movement. When the assembly is top dead centre, the power to the horizonal axis is 0, and the Y axis moves freely. As you rotate the assembly clockwise, more and more of the power is given to the X axis until at 90 degrees, all of the power is delivered to the X stroke.

replies.author: @1992jamo
@1992jamo
replies.text: @areadenial2343 So rather than it being a clutch where you have input rotation, and output rotation based on friction and slipping. You have input rotation, and output movement back and forth, which can easily be transferred back into rotational movement. The different being though that rather than the power being delivered by friction, it's delivered by increasing movement given to an axis.

replies.author: @1992jamo
replies.text:
@areadenial2343 You'd only need one of these mechanisms. As you rotated the ring gear, more of the movement is available in the X axis. The X axis carriage has the same frequency, but the movement length/stroke increases.
All that is left is to convert the linear movement back into rotational.

author: @YodaWhat
text: In the 1980s I saw video of a MUCH simpler *geared CVT* that I hoped to find in this video, based on the name. That old one was invented by an Australian mathematician and publicly demonstrated. Unfortunately, I do not remember his name or the exact name of his device, but I think it was called an "epicyclic transmission" or something similar. The particular version I saw had ratios continuously variable from 6:1 speed increase all the way to some absurdly large speed reduction like 1:1000, if I recall correctly. When the simple control lever was pushed _beyond maximum reduction,_ the transmission disengaged, so it doubled as a clutch. Given the tremendous speed reduction, that clutch action would have been smoother than most friction clutches. The basic way that transmission was described as working was to take _the near-circular portion_ of epicycloids, cut them out, and re-assemble them into complete curves. I wish somebody could remember the details more clearly, including the inventor's name.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would be helpful if you did remember that i would be very interested to know

replies.author: @jamieoxenreider
replies.text: I kind of remember. If it is what I remember it uses a small servo motor to counter turn the sun gear and the speed it was counter turned changed the ratio and it hade 4 planets that  were nontypical shaped gears. Advice: drop it, you will waste your life pursuing this kind of stuff. There already is cvts that were bad but now work just fine. Even if you invented one, the ones out there are already good and super simple and cheap to make.

author: @Bapate-rh9be
text: Tiny tipp: All polymeric materials have terrible friction onto themselfes and chemically similar materials, but excellent friction on other, not chemically similar materials. If you print 1 part from PLA and the other one from ABS or PET-G or similar you get a very good, self lubricating bearing. Basically ABS & ASA, PET-G and PLA can be used as a triangle as they all have good friction on each other, allowing you to get away with quite a lot. Keep to unreinforced materials. Polyamide has the best fricition - however unreinforced PA prints horribly. There is also special self lubricating materials like the ones beeing sold by igus - they are expensive but you only need medicinal amounts.

replies.author: @yodaco
replies.text: If the materials carry the same charge this also has an effect

author: @st0rmerc00l8
text:
Genius system, I never thought about changing the phase of a hypercycloidal gear by moving the usually locked outer ring with a worm gear.
I can defintely see potential in a CVT design made from this principle being backdrivable and effecient!

But... This mechanism still produces reciprocating motion (its adjustable with a simple mechanism which is brilliant) and converting that reciprocating motion back into rotary is the main challenge with most of these potential CVT systems sadly.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: glad you liked it mate

replies.author: @jamieoxenreider
replies.text: It's actually pretty simple to do. I invented it years ago but always assumed it was already patented. You use a rack gear with two spur gears with (one way bearings also called free wheels.) If you don't know what those are you will find it handy,. they are basically like a bicycle in that they lock pone direction and free spin in the other.

replies.author: @st0rmerc00l8
replies.text:
Unfortunately, one way bearings prevent backdriving unless you double up the mechanism with one going one way and the other in reverse, which adds to much complexity and weight for most designs.

The type of ideal CVT would have high torque capabilities, non friction force transfers, a high gear ratio range, be backdrivable and relatively small. - This is sadly nearly impossible to do unless some new mechanism is invented.

I do really want to see these systems work, but we must address any flaws!

replies.author: @jamieoxenreider
replies.text: Yeah absolutely you have to have two for either direction and possibly a third for overrunning which automatics already use a freewheel for over running. Not complex at all.

author: @Berkana
text:
Robert, if you use two of these with a phase difference of 90 degrees and then translate the linear movement back to rotation, there would be no dead zones in the rotation. That would let you put rotation in and get rotation out. 

Or, you could even go three-phase with this.

replies.author: @IlluminatedFeline
replies.text: Us two that are connected with offset angles to one-another? Like an offset herringbone gear to minimize backlash?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice - cheers mate

replies.author: @Berkana
replies.text: @IlluminatedFeline Not sure what the physical arrangement would be, but this conversion between continuous rotation and linear sine wave is analogous to AC electricity. If you want smooth power output, you can use 3-phase AC where each sine wave is 120˚ phase shifted from the prior. Three of these waves driving an AC motor gives you smooth rotational output. So if you have an input rotation that turns into three sine waves, and use those to convert back to rotation, that could let you have rotation going in and rotation coming out of the device. I don't think this would change the output speed though, so it might not be sufficient to be a CVT.

replies.author: @pgtmr2713
replies.text: @Berkana Rotation in and rotation out, sounds like wasting energy, unless there's a speed or tq or reversing direction reason.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @wanglydiaplt
text: Hello! I think I first came across this inside an old South Bend shaper which had the ability to change stroke length with a crank on top of the slide. I searched for ages to find another similar mechanism and that's when I found the one on grabcad. At the moment I'm working on another version that hopefully will work with lower friction in order to make a longer lasting motor driven version but eventually it would be great to make another 'real' one to do something useful in the workshop; possibly a die filer or a vertically mounted sawblade by first turning the who mechanism on its side. 🙂
